content.text: ES Modules (ESM) are the official JavaScript module system, standardised in ES2015 and now used natively in browsers and Node.js. Every React project you will encounter uses ESM — the

keywords you see in component files are ESM syntax. Understanding how modules work — what gets exported, how imports are resolved, and the difference between named and default exports — is foundational for reading and writing React code confidently.
A module is a file. In ES Modules, every
) file that contains at least one
statement is treated as a module. Variables and functions inside a module are
— they are not accessible from other files unless explicitly exported. This is the opposite of classic
tags, where everything leaked into the global scope.

attrs.alt: ES Modules diagram showing named exports, default exports, namespace imports, and barrel re-exports across multiple files
attrs.title: ES Modules — Export and Import Patterns

content.text: Named exports let a module expose any number of values, each identified by the name it was declared with. The name must be used exactly when importing, unless you create an alias.
A module can have at most one default export. It represents the primary thing the module provides — typically a class or function. The importer can name it anything; no curly braces are needed.
A module can have both a default export and named exports simultaneously. React itself uses this pattern extensively.
syntax imports everything a module exports as properties of a single object. This is a namespace import.
Namespace imports can make code more readable when a module exports many things — you can tell at a glance that
comes from the math utilities module. However, bundlers like Vite and Webpack can tree-shake named imports more aggressively than namespace imports.
Every React component file is an ES Module. The component function is typically the default export; hooks, utilities, and types are named exports.
A barrel file re-exports from several modules in a directory through a single
entry point. This is a common React project pattern that lets consumers import from the directory rather than individual files.

Barrel files can reduce the effectiveness of tree-shaking if your bundler cannot statically analyse which re-exports are used. Keep barrels shallow and avoid re-exporting everything from deeply nested directories in large codebases.
declarations must appear at the top level and cannot be conditional. When you need to load a module lazily — based on a user action or a route — use dynamic
wraps dynamic import to enable code splitting — large components are loaded only when they are first rendered, reducing the initial bundle size.
statement tells the runtime where to find the module.
) — resolved relative to the importing file. This is what you use for your own code.
by the bundler (Vite, Webpack) or Node.js. The package

to a specific directory, avoiding long relative paths like
Understanding these properties prevents common bugs:
of a module runs its top-level code. Subsequent imports of the same module get the cached result — the module does not run again.
Named imports are live read-only bindings to the exporting module's variables. If the exported value changes inside the exporting module, all importers see the new value.
ESM code always runs in strict mode —
is implicit. Certain loose syntax (undeclared variables, duplicate parameter names) that works in scripts throws a SyntaxError in modules.
statements must be at the top level of the file. They cannot be inside
blocks, loops, or functions. This restriction allows bundlers to statically analyse the import graph and remove unused code (tree-shaking).
) was Node.js's original module system. React projects use ESM, but older tooling and many npm packages still ship CommonJS. Bundlers handle the interop transparently in most cases.
Curly braces vs no curly braces
The most frequent source of confusion is forgetting when to use curly braces.
ESM supports circular imports (A imports B, B imports A), but you can get

values at runtime if a module tries to use an import before the other module has finished initialising. The fix is usually to restructure so the shared dependency lives in a third module that neither imports the other.
When running ES Modules in Node.js without a bundler, import paths require explicit file extensions (
). In Vite-based React projects the bundler resolves extensions automatically, so you will see both styles in the wild.
function, at the top level of a module. When a module uses top-level
, any module that imports from it will wait for it to finish before executing. This is only available in ES Modules, not CommonJS.
sparingly. It delays all downstream imports and can make startup behaviour hard to reason about. In React apps, it is more common to load data inside components or custom hooks.
Organising a React project with modules
A consistent module organisation makes large React codebases maintainable. A common pattern groups files by feature, with each feature directory containing its own barrel file.

src/
├── components/          # Shared UI components
│   ├── Button/
│   │   ├── Button.jsx
│   │   ├── Button.test.jsx
│   │   └── index.js     # re-exports Button as default
│   └── index.js         # barrel: re-exports all components
├── hooks/               # Custom React hooks
│   ├── useLocalStorage.js
│   ├── useDebounce.js
│   └── index.js
├── utils/               # Pure utility functions
│   ├── format.js
│   └── validators.js
└── features/
    ├── auth/
    │   ├── AuthProvider.jsx
    │   ├── useAuth.js
    │   └── index.js
    └── dashboard/
        ├── Dashboard.jsx
        └── index.js
Splitting logic into modules: a worked example
Suppose you are building a shopping cart feature. You can split responsibilities across modules so each file has a single, clear purpose.
Each module imports only what it needs.
is testable with React Testing Library;
focuses on presentation. This is the power of modules: each boundary is enforced by the file system.
Vite, the default bundler for new React projects, adds several ESM-adjacent features worth knowing.
Vite lets you import SVGs, images, and CSS files as ES modules —
gives you the resolved URL string.
lets you import all files matching a pattern — useful for auto-loading routes or fixtures.

In development, Vite uses native browser ESM to serve files individually. When you save a file, only that module and its dependents are updated — not the entire bundle. This is why Vite's dev server starts instantly.
React projects have established conventions for default vs named exports that are worth following.
React component (the main thing in a file)
One of the biggest advantages of splitting code into modules is testability. Vitest and Jest both support ESM natively. Utility modules (no React) can be tested with zero setup.
Mocking modules is also straightforward with Vitest —
replaces the entire module with a mock so you can test components without hitting real network endpoints.
is an ES Module; its identifiers are private unless exported.
